title: The Day of the Lord and the New Heavens
subtitle: Scoffers dismissed, divine patience explained, cosmic renewal promised

heb.paragraph: Scoffers (empaiktai) will come in the last days - those who ridicule the promise of Christ's return. Mockery is a sign of the end.
Following their own sinful desires - the mockers are not intellectually honest skeptics but morally motivated deniers.

heb.gloss: to bring together, hold together, constitute
heb.paragraph: By the word of God heavens existed and earth was formed (synestosa) - creation was by divine speech; so will be destruction.

refs.note: In the last time there will be scoffers, following their own ungodly passions.
God said, Let the waters be gathered.
The fountains of the great deep burst forth.
Of old you laid the foundation of the earth.

notes.note: This is my second letter to stir up your sincere mind, to remember prophetic words and the apostolic commandment.
Scoffers will come in the last days, mocking: Where is the promise of his coming? Everything continues as from creation.
They deliberately overlook that by God's word heavens and earth were formed from water, and the world perished by water. Present heavens and earth are stored for fire.
Calvin notes that uniformitarianism (all continues as always) is not a new argument. The flood refutes it; fire awaits.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: All things continue as they were from creation - this is philosophical uniformitarianism, denying divine intervention.
Deliberately overlook (lanthano) - the ignorance is willful, not accidental. They suppress inconvenient truth.
Stored up for fire - the destruction will be by fire, not water. The same powerful word that created will destroy.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that the scoffers are not neutral skeptics but morally compromised. Their mockery serves their lifestyle.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes that Peter uses the flood as precedent. If God intervened once, he can and will intervene again.

hist.historical: Second Peter addresses the threat of false teachers who deny Christ's return (parousia) and promote moral license. The author presents himself as the apostle Peter writing shortly before his death (1:14), though many scholars date the letter later due to its developed theology, reference to Paul's letters as 'Scripture' (3:16), and literary dependence on Jude. The false teachers mock the delay of Christ's return ('Where is the promise of his coming?' 3:4) and use this skepticism to justify immoral living. The letter defends apostolic eyewitness testimony (1:16-18), warns of judgment using Old Testament examples (2:4-10), and explains that God's patience allows time for repentance (3:9).
hist.context: Peter writes to stir up sincere minds, reminding them of prophetic words and apostolic commands. In the last days scoffers will come, mocking the promise of Christ's coming. They ask: Where is the promise? All continues as from creation. They deliberately overlook that by God's word heavens existed and earth was formed from water, and that the ancient world was destroyed by water. By the same word, present heavens and earth are stored up for fire, kept for judgment day and destruction of the ungodly.

heb.gloss: to be patient, slow to anger
heb.paragraph: The Lord is patient (makrothumei) toward you - delay is not inability but patience. He desires all to reach repentance.
The day of the Lord will come like a thief - unexpected, unannounced. Readiness is essential.
New heavens and a new earth (kainos) - not merely renewed but qualitatively different, where righteousness dwells.

refs.note: A thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday.
The day of the Lord will come like a thief in the night.
Behold, I create new heavens and a new earth.
I saw a new heaven and a new earth.

notes.note: With the Lord, one day is as a thousand years. The Lord is not slow but patient, not wishing any to perish but all to reach repentance.
The day of the Lord will come like a thief. Heavens will pass away with a roar; elements will be burned; earth and its works exposed.
Since all will be dissolved, what sort of people ought you to be? Waiting for and hastening the day. We wait for new heavens and new earth where righteousness dwells.
Calvin sees the delay as mercy. God gives time for repentance. The offer remains open; patience is not indifference.
The dissolution of all things should produce holiness, not despair. New creation is the goal; present conduct prepares for it.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Not wishing any to perish - the desire is universal, though the accomplishment is particular. God's heart is for all.
Elements (stoicheia) may mean basic components or elemental spirits. Either way, cosmic dissolution is comprehensive.
Hastening (speudo) the day - believers may hasten the parousia through holy living and mission. Their conduct affects timing.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes eschatological ethics. The certainty of cosmic dissolution should produce holiness, not nihilism.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes the tension between divine sovereignty (the day will come) and human responsibility (hastening). Both are affirmed.

hist.context: Peter addresses the apparent delay. With the Lord, one day is as a thousand years and a thousand years as one day. The Lord is not slow as some count slowness but is patient, not wishing any to perish but all to reach repentance. The day of the Lord will come like a thief; heavens will pass away with a roar, elements will be burned, earth and its works exposed. Since all will be dissolved, what sort of people ought you to be in holiness and godliness, waiting and hastening the coming day? According to his promise, we wait for new heavens and new earth where righteousness dwells.

heb.paragraph: Be diligent (spoudasate) to be found by him without spot or blemish and at peace - effort is required for readiness.
Without spot or blemish (aspiloi kai amometoi) - the opposite of the false teachers who are spots and blemishes (2:13).
Grow in grace and knowledge - steadfastness prevents being carried away by error. Growth is the antidote to apostasy.

refs.note: Do you presume on the riches of his kindness and patience?
No longer be children, tossed by every wind of doctrine.
Walk in a manner worthy of the Lord, bearing fruit and growing in knowledge.
To him who is able to keep you from stumbling.

notes.note: Be diligent to be found without spot or blemish, at peace. Count the patience of our Lord as salvation.
As our beloved brother Paul wrote with wisdom given him. His letters have hard things that the ignorant twist to their destruction.
You know this beforehand, so guard yourselves. Do not be carried away by error. Grow in grace and knowledge. To him be glory forever.
Calvin notes that Peter acknowledges Paul's letters as Scripture. The ignorant twist them, but the letters themselves are authoritative.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Beloved brother Paul - remarkable given the tension between Peter and Paul at Antioch. Here is reconciliation and respect.
Other Scriptures (loipas graphas) - Peter places Paul's letters alongside Scripture. This is early evidence of canonical recognition.
Grow in grace and knowledge - the double object emphasizes both divine empowerment (grace) and intellectual grasp (knowledge).

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes the canonical significance: Peter treats Paul's letters as Scripture. The NT is recognizing itself.

davids.source: Peter Davids, NICNT/Pillar 2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Davids notes that the doxology is Christological. Glory belongs to Jesus Christ now and forever. The letter ends as it began - focused on Christ.

hist.context: Peter exhorts believers to be diligent, to be found at peace, without spot or blemish. They should count divine patience as salvation, as beloved Paul also wrote with wisdom given him. Paul's letters contain hard things that the ignorant and unstable twist, as they do other Scriptures, to their destruction. Therefore, knowing this beforehand, guard yourselves lest you be carried away by the error of lawless people and lose stability. But grow in grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be glory both now and to the day of eternity. Amen.

lit.note: Chapter 3 addresses eschatological skepticism, explains divine patience, and promises cosmic renewal.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates: God controls time and will fulfill his promise. Judgment appears in fire reserved for the ungodly. Mercy in divine patience.
